questions.stem: This Prussian-blue-stained marrow shows the arrowed cell with a ring of blue iron granules encircling the nucleus. What is this cell?

explanation.summary: A ringed sideroblast is an erythroid precursor with iron-laden mitochondria forming a perinuclear ring (Prussian blue positive), diagnostic of sideroblastic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: A ringed sideroblast is an erythroid precursor with iron-laden mitochondria forming a perinuclear ring (Prussian blue positive), diagnostic of sideroblastic anemia.

questions.stem: In this comparison, total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) moves opposite to serum iron, rising in iron deficiency and falling in overload. What accounts for the rise in TIBC when iron is deficient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The liver increases transferrin synthesis, raising the plasma capacity to bind iron

choices.label: B
choices.text: Red cells release stored transferrin into plasma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Macrophages secrete transferrin during iron overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ferritin is converted into circulating transferrin

explanation.summary: TIBC reflects circulating transferrin. In iron deficiency the liver upregulates transferrin production to scavenge scarce iron, so TIBC rises; in iron overload transferrin synthesis is suppressed and TIBC falls.
explanation.whyCorrect: TIBC reflects circulating transferrin. In iron deficiency the liver upregulates transferrin production to scavenge scarce iron, so TIBC rises; in iron overload transferrin synthesis is suppressed and TIBC falls.

questions.stem: In this table of iron studies, row C shows increased serum iron, increased ferritin, decreased TIBC, and increased transferrin saturation. Which clinical condition does this pattern represent?

explanation.summary: Iron overload raises serum iron, ferritin (storage), and transferrin saturation, while suppressing transferrin synthesis so TIBC falls. This high-saturation, high-ferritin, low-TIBC pattern is characteristic of hemochromatosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Iron overload raises serum iron, ferritin (storage), and transferrin saturation, while suppressing transferrin synthesis so TIBC falls. This high-saturation, high-ferritin, low-TIBC pattern is characteristic of hemochromatosis.

questions.stem: The dark reddish-brown 'port-wine' urine on the right (compared with normal urine on the left) develops in porphyrias. Excretion of which substances produces this color change?

explanation.summary: Overproduced porphyrin precursors are excreted in urine and darken to a port-wine color on air/light exposure. This finding accompanies several porphyrias and reflects excess heme-pathway intermediates spilling into the urine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Overproduced porphyrin precursors are excreted in urine and darken to a port-wine color on air/light exposure. This finding accompanies several porphyrias and reflects excess heme-pathway intermediates spilling into the urine.

questions.stem: This intraoperative image shows a strikingly dark, greenish-black liver. This appearance is characteristic of which inherited hyperbilirubinemia?

explanation.summary: Dubin-Johnson syndrome (defective MRP2/canalicular transport of conjugated bilirubin) causes accumulation of dark pigment giving the liver a grossly black appearance, with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dubin-Johnson syndrome (defective MRP2/canalicular transport of conjugated bilirubin) causes accumulation of dark pigment giving the liver a grossly black appearance, with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.

questions.stem: A middle-aged patient with new-onset diabetes and cirrhosis develops this diffuse bronze-brown skin discoloration. Which disorder best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: Iron overload from hemochromatosis deposits in skin and stimulates melanin production, producing the classic bronze/slate-gray hyperpigmentation. The combination of skin pigmentation, diabetes, and cirrhosis is the classic 'bronze diabetes' triad.
explanation.whyCorrect: Iron overload from hemochromatosis deposits in skin and stimulates melanin production, producing the classic bronze/slate-gray hyperpigmentation. The combination of skin pigmentation, diabetes, and cirrhosis is the classic 'bronze diabetes' triad.

questions.stem: This pathway highlights a block at porphobilinogen deaminase, causing accumulation of ALA and porphobilinogen. What is the classic clinical presentation of the disorder produced by this enzyme deficiency?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute neurovisceral attacks with abdominal pain and neuropsychiatric symptoms

explanation.summary: Deficiency of porphobilinogen deaminase causes acute intermittent porphyria. Buildup of ALA and porphobilinogen produces episodic abdominal pain, neuropathy, and neuropsychiatric changes, typically without photosensitivity since no photosensitizing porphyrins accumulate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Deficiency of porphobilinogen deaminase causes acute intermittent porphyria. Buildup of ALA and porphobilinogen produces episodic abdominal pain, neuropathy, and neuropsychiatric changes, typically without photosensitivity since no photosensitizing porphyrins accumulate.

questions.stem: This inset marks a deficiency of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase, the defect in the most common porphyria. How does this disorder typically present clinically?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recurrent acute abdominal pain attacks without skin findings

choices.label: C
choices.text: Photosensitive blistering and fragility of sun-exposed skin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hepatic cirrhosis with no cutaneous signs

explanation.summary: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency causes porphyria cutanea tarda, the most common porphyria. Accumulated photosensitizing uroporphyrins deposit in skin, producing blistering, fragility, and hyperpigmentation on sun-exposed areas; it is often associated with liver disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency causes porphyria cutanea tarda, the most common porphyria. Accumulated photosensitizing uroporphyrins deposit in skin, producing blistering, fragility, and hyperpigmentation on sun-exposed areas; it is often associated with liver disease.

questions.stem: These vesicles, erosions, hemorrhagic crusts, and scarring on the sun-exposed dorsal hands are characteristic of which condition?

explanation.summary: Porphyria cutanea tarda causes skin fragility and blistering on sun-exposed skin (especially the dorsal hands) due to photosensitizing uroporphyrins from uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency. Lesions heal with scarring, milia, and hyperpigmentation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Porphyria cutanea tarda causes skin fragility and blistering on sun-exposed skin (especially the dorsal hands) due to photosensitizing uroporphyrins from uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency. Lesions heal with scarring, milia, and hyperpigmentation.

questions.stem: In this degradation sequence, the enzyme that opens the porphyrin ring of heme, releasing carbon monoxide and Fe2+ while forming biliverdin, is which one?

explanation.summary: Heme oxygenase cleaves the alpha-methene bridge of heme, releasing CO (the only endogenous source) and Fe2+ and producing green biliverdin, which is then reduced to bilirubin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heme oxygenase cleaves the alpha-methene bridge of heme, releasing CO (the only endogenous source) and Fe2+ and producing green biliverdin, which is then reduced to bilirubin.

questions.stem: This overview traces senescent red cell breakdown. Which liver enzyme conjugates bilirubin with UDP-glucuronate to make it water soluble for biliary excretion?

explanation.summary: Hepatic UGT1A1 conjugates lipophilic unconjugated bilirubin with glucuronate, yielding water-soluble bilirubin diglucuronide that is secreted into bile.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatic UGT1A1 conjugates lipophilic unconjugated bilirubin with glucuronate, yielding water-soluble bilirubin diglucuronide that is secreted into bile.

questions.stem: This jaundiced neonate is being treated with the blue light source shown. What is the primary purpose of this therapy?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Warm the infant to prevent hypothermia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sterilize the skin to prevent sepsis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Photoisomerize unconjugated bilirubin into water-soluble isomers for excretion

explanation.summary: Phototherapy converts lipophilic unconjugated bilirubin in the skin into water-soluble photoisomers (lumirubin) that can be excreted in bile and urine without hepatic conjugation, lowering the risk of kernicterus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phototherapy converts lipophilic unconjugated bilirubin in the skin into water-soluble photoisomers (lumirubin) that can be excreted in bile and urine without hepatic conjugation, lowering the risk of kernicterus.

questions.stem: This schematic explains why blue light (~460-490 nm) is used clinically in jaundiced newborns. By what mechanism does it lower bilirubin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It induces hepatic UGT1A1 conjugation enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: It converts bilirubin into water-soluble structural isomers (lumirubin) excreted in bile and urine

choices.label: C
choices.text: It oxidizes bilirubin directly to carbon dioxide

choices.label: E
choices.text: It breaks down albumin to release bound bilirubin

explanation.summary: Blue light near 460-490 nm is absorbed by bilirubin, generating water-soluble photoisomers such as Z-lumirubin that are excreted in bile and urine without conjugation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Blue light near 460-490 nm is absorbed by bilirubin, generating water-soluble photoisomers such as Z-lumirubin that are excreted in bile and urine without conjugation.

questions.stem: In this heme synthesis diagram, lead inhibits the two enzymes marked with red bars. Which pair of enzymes does lead poisoning classically block?

explanation.summary: Lead inhibits ALA dehydratase (aminolevulinic acid dehydrase) and ferrochelatase, causing accumulation of ALA and protoporphyrin and the anemia of lead poisoning.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lead inhibits ALA dehydratase (aminolevulinic acid dehydrase) and ferrochelatase, causing accumulation of ALA and protoporphyrin and the anemia of lead poisoning.

questions.stem: This diagram depicts the first, committed, and rate-limiting reaction of a biosynthetic pathway, in which glycine condenses with succinyl-CoA. Which pathway is being initiated?

explanation.summary: The condensation of glycine and succinyl-CoA by ALA synthase to form delta-aminolevulinic acid is the rate-limiting first step of heme synthesis. The hepatic isoform (ALAS1) is feedback-inhibited by heme, while the erythroid isoform (ALAS2) is stimulated by intracellular iron.
explanation.whyCorrect: The condensation of glycine and succinyl-CoA by ALA synthase to form delta-aminolevulinic acid is the rate-limiting first step of heme synthesis. The hepatic isoform (ALAS1) is feedback-inhibited by heme, while the erythroid isoform (ALAS2) is stimulated by intracellular iron.

questions.stem: In the heme synthesis pathway shown, the initial enzyme is stimulated by alcohol, barbiturates, and hypoxia. Precipitation of attacks by such inducers is a hallmark of which category of disease?

explanation.summary: Inducers of ALA synthase (alcohol, barbiturates, hypoxia, low glucose) increase flux through the heme pathway and can precipitate acute porphyria attacks. The inset also shows that a ferrochelatase block causes erythropoietic protoporphyria.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inducers of ALA synthase (alcohol, barbiturates, hypoxia, low glucose) increase flux through the heme pathway and can precipitate acute porphyria attacks. The inset also shows that a ferrochelatase block causes erythropoietic protoporphyria.

questions.stem: This diagram tracks the distribution and daily turnover of body iron across tissues, with only ~1 mg lost per day through feces, urine, sweat, and skin. What key principle about iron does it emphasize?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The body has no regulated mechanism for actively excreting excess iron

choices.label: B
choices.text: Iron cannot be recycled once incorporated into hemoglobin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most body iron is stored in the liver as ferritin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dietary iron is absorbed with nearly 100% efficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron is freely excreted by the kidney when in excess

explanation.summary: Because iron losses are small and unregulated (~1 mg/day), total body iron is controlled almost entirely at the level of intestinal absorption. Most iron is in circulating hemoglobin, and macrophages recycle iron from senescent red cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because iron losses are small and unregulated (~1 mg/day), total body iron is controlled almost entirely at the level of intestinal absorption. Most iron is in circulating hemoglobin, and macrophages recycle iron from senescent red cells.

questions.stem: In this model of intestinal iron handling, the liver-derived hormone acting on ferroportin 1 (red inhibitory bar) is a master regulator of iron. What is the net effect of increasing this hormone?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased iron absorption into the bloodstream

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased release of stored iron from macrophages

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased iron export into the blood, lowering serum iron

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enhanced reduction of dietary Fe3+ to Fe2+

explanation.summary: Hepcidin binds and degrades ferroportin, blocking iron export from enterocytes and macrophages. High hepcidin (e.g., in inflammation) traps iron in cells and lowers serum iron, contributing to anemia of chronic disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepcidin binds and degrades ferroportin, blocking iron export from enterocytes and macrophages. High hepcidin (e.g., in inflammation) traps iron in cells and lowers serum iron, contributing to anemia of chronic disease.

questions.stem: This diagram illustrates how ferritin translation is controlled by iron. By what mechanism does a rise in intracellular iron increase ferritin production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Iron binds the IRE directly, recruiting ribosomes to the mRNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Iron binds the iron-response element binding protein (IRE-BP), causing it to release from the IRE so the mRNA can be translated

choices.label: C
choices.text: Iron stabilizes ferritin mRNA against degradation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Iron phosphorylates ferritin protein to activate it

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron increases transcription of the ferritin gene in the nucleus

explanation.summary: When iron is low, IRE-BP occupies the 5' IRE and blocks translation. When iron is abundant, it binds IRE-BP, which releases the IRE, permitting ribosome loading and ferritin synthesis so excess iron can be safely stored.
explanation.whyCorrect: When iron is low, IRE-BP occupies the 5' IRE and blocks translation. When iron is abundant, it binds IRE-BP, which releases the IRE, permitting ribosome loading and ferritin synthesis so excess iron can be safely stored.

questions.stem: This diagram shows that in the low-iron state, IRP binds the IREs on transferrin receptor (TfR) mRNA, stabilizing it and increasing TfR production. Under this same low-iron condition, what happens to ferritin synthesis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ferritin synthesis increases in parallel with TfR

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ferritin is exported from the cell instead of stored

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ferritin mRNA is degraded but protein rises

explanation.summary: The same iron-regulatory proteins reciprocally control these mRNAs. In iron deficiency, IRP binding stabilizes TfR mRNA (more receptors to import iron) but blocks translation of ferritin mRNA (less storage protein), coordinating uptake and storage inversely.
explanation.whyCorrect: The same iron-regulatory proteins reciprocally control these mRNAs. In iron deficiency, IRP binding stabilizes TfR mRNA (more receptors to import iron) but blocks translation of ferritin mRNA (less storage protein), coordinating uptake and storage inversely.
